dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 dispatched for a 67-year-old female with escalating tachypnea and respiratory distress.
Hello, my name is Jordan, I'm an EMT. What made you call 911 today?
I just can't seem to breathe right. It got worse today.
You appear alert. Can you tell me your name?
Your eyes are open and looking around clearly.
Can you tell me the date today?
Great. Can you lift your arm for me?
Your airway is patent and clear. I see no signs of obstruction.
You're breathing very fast and shallow. I hear some wheezing when I listen.
You're experiencing tachypnea. How long has this been going on?
It started this morning. I couldn't get relief with my inhalers.
Yes. She's tachypneic with respirations noted at 34-rapid and shallow, with audible wheezing.
I also observe accessory muscle use during each breath.
Her initial SpO2 was 91%, which improved to 99% after an oxygen increase.
Pulse is strong at 109-clearly elevated. Skin is warm, dry, and pink with good capillary refill.
Blood pressure was 165/115 initially, then increased to 172/119. We've increased oxygen to 6 LPM via nasal cannula.
Let's learn more about your history. Do you have any long-term health conditions?
She has a longstanding history of COPD, emphysema, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteoporosis, GERD, and depression.
What medications are you currently using?
I take albuterol, prednisone, Coumadin, oxygen, and a few others.
She is allergic to penicillin, naproxen, and amoxicillin.
The home nurse increased her oxygen to 4LPM this morning. It has not helped.
How would you rate the difficulty you're having breathing on a scale of 0 to 10?
About a 7. It feels like parts of my lung just aren't working.
Eyes show jaundiced sclera. No fluid from the nose or ears. Pupils are equal and reactive at 3mm.
I see surgical scars on your back. Have you had spinal surgery before?
Yes, I had spinal fusion several years ago.
You're in a tripod position for comfort. We'll keep you in this position.
We will not be able to obtain glucose at this time, per ePCR.
Heart rate remains at 109 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate started at 34, improved to 24, and is now at 26.
Blood pressure was 165/115 initially, then 172/119, and is now 182/78.
Oxygen saturation improved to 99% after increasing oxygen to 6LPM via nasal cannula.
EKG is showing a steady sinus tachycardia. No ectopic beats or ST segment deviations.
We're going to start an IV to administer medications.
IV access successfully achieved in the right wrist with a 22G catheter. Site is secure with occlusive dressing.
We're administering DuoNeb with albuterol and ipratropium to open your airways per protocol.
We've also given you 10mg of Dexamethasone intravenously to help with breathing, per ePCR.
Normal saline is also running to support you as an additional fluid.
EKG has again confirmed a sinus tachycardia without other significant findings.
What are the rest of your medications?
I also take benazepril, amlodipine, Fluticasone, pantoprazole, and sertraline.
I also take atorvastatin, Pulmicort, and Percocet as well.
We're continuing nebulizer treatment with DuoNeb as per protocol.
A 4-lead EKG is now running and verifying sinus tachycardia.
A 12-lead EKG was obtained showing possible anterior infarct and lateral ischemic findings on lead aVL/V1/V2.
Breath sounds are diminished and inspiratory and expiratory wheezes are audible in all lung fields.
Breathing is at 26 now, with a bit more depth.
Blood pressure remains elevated at 182/78 but stable.
Oxygen saturation remains steady at 99%.
We just completed a second cycle of DuoNeb per protocol.
Did you take any medications in the last 24 hours?
I took my inhalers and a dose of prednisone.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-alert and oriented to person, place, event.
You are not reporting pain currently.
The respiratory distress protocol 3-11 is being closely followed for COPD exacerbation, per ePCR.
Pulse is currently at 105-trending stable.
SpO2 continues to remain at a stable 99%.
After the second medication with DuoNeb, your breathing improved some.
IV site remains patent with no signs of infiltration or bleeding.
You said it was a 7. Is that still accurate for your breathing?
Yes, it's better but I still feel restricted.
Was there a specific trigger for the worsening breathing this morning?
She was gardening briefly, sat down, and then it progressively worsened.
Blood pressure remains at 182/78.
Where would you like to be taken for further care?
VCU Hospital. They know how to handle this.
We are transporting you to the hospital to get better care. We'll make sure you stay comfortable and secure.

67-year-old female with worsening COPD exacerbation and tachypnea. Administered nebulized DuoNeb with albuterol and ipratropium, 10mg dexamethasone IV, and 12-lead EKG showing lateral ischemic and anterior infarct indications.
